text.fullText: Docusign Envelope ID: 34F80D10-74E9-42B7-A2AF-91F3BBD33BCA Department of Environmental Conservation Agency of Natural Resources Watershed Management Division 1 National Life Drive, Davis 3 [phone] 802-828-1115 Montpelier, Vermont 05620-3522 https://dec.vermont.gov/watershed February 17, 2026 Via Electronic Submission on Regulations.gov and email: cwa401@epa.gov. Administrator Lee Zeldin and Lauren Kasparek Environmental Protection Agency Oceans, Wetlands, and Communities Division, Office of Water (4504-T) 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue, NW Washington, DC 20460 RE: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Dear Administrator Zeldin and Ms. Kasparek: The Vermont Department of Environmental Conservation (“VTDEC”) respectfully submits the following comments in response to Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929. VTDEC’s comments focus on the four issues central to the proposed rule: (1) scope of certification; (2) cooperative federalism and state sovereignty; (3) request for certification; and (4) modifications. VTDEC is the primary agency responsible for administering water quality standards and protecting surface waters within the State of Vermont. The State has a substantial interest in ensuring that any final rule fully comports with the text, structure, and purpose of the Clean Water Act (“CWA”) and preserves the cooperative federalism framework Congress established. I. Scope of Certification The Environmental Protection Agency’s (“the EPA” or “the Agency”) proposed definition of “water quality requirements” reflects the EPA’s proposed interpretation of the statutory phrase “other appropriate requirements of State law” as “State or Tribal regulatory requirements for point source discharges into waters of the United States.” This interpretation is unduly narrow and inconsistent with both the statutory text of Section 401 and longstanding judicial and regulatory precedent. EPA’s proposal would fundamentally restructure the State’s role under Section 401 by narrowing the substantive scope of certification, restricting state procedural authority, and shifting regulatory power away from certifying authorities and towards applicants. Reviewing underlying licensed activities is essential as they determine the nature, magnitude, duration, and location of any resulting discharge. Page 1 of 7 Docusign Envelope ID: 34F80D10-74E9-42B7-A2AF-91F3BBD33BCA The Vermont State Legislature has expressly recognized this principle and codified it in State law. Under 10 V.S.A. § 1253(h)(1), VTDEC is required to review: “activities that require a federal license or permit…to ensure that a proposed activity complies with the Vermont Water Quality Standards, as well as with any other appropriate requirement of State law . . . .” Those requirements of Vermont state law include: (1) 10 V.S.A. chapter 41 (regulation of stream flow); (2) 10 V.S.A. § 1264 (stormwater management); (3) 29 V.S.A. chapter 11 (management of lakes and ponds); and (4) The Agency of Natural Resources Rules for Water Withdrawals for Snowmaking. 10 V.S.A. § 1253(h)(1). These statutory directives enable Vermont to implement its obligations under Section 401 and reflect the State’s authority to protect its waters through substantive review of federally licensed activities. Moreover, Vermont Water Quality Standards, which establish designated uses, management objectives, narrative and numeric water quality criteria, and the State’s antidegradation policy were adopted through State-mandated public processes and, critically, have been reviewed and approved by the EPA pursuant to the CWA section 303.1 Accordingly, the Vermont Water Quality Standards constitute both “water quality requirements” under federal law and binding requirements under Vermont law. Limiting “water quality requirements” to only numeric water quality criteria would contradict longstanding federal law recognizing that narrative criteria, designated uses, and antidegradation policies are enforceable water quality standards under the CWA. As held by the United States Supreme Court, “limitations to assure compliance with state water quality standards are also permitted by § 401(d)’s reference to ‘any other appropriate requirement of State law. . . .[A]t a minimum, limitations imposed pursuant to state water quality standards…are ‘appropriate’ requirements of state law.” PUD No. 1 Jefferson Cnty v. Washington Dept. of Ecology, 511 U.S. 700, 713-18 (1994) (PUD No. 1) (The Court continued on to discuss 33 U.S.C. § 1313(a), holding “[b]y providing in 1972 that existing state water quality standards would remain in force until revised, the Clean Water Act ensured that the States would continue their antidegradation programs.”) The Court further confirmed that “[s]tates may impose standards on the discharge of pollutants that are stricter than federal ones.” S.D. Warren Co. v. Maine Bd. of Environmental Protection, 547 U.S. 370, 386 (2006). Federal regulations likewise define water quality standards to include designated uses, narrative and numeric criteria, and antidegradation policies. See, 40 C.F.R. §§ 131.3, 131.6, and 131.11 1 See Current Vermont Water Quality Standards, Vermont Agency of Natural Resources, Department of Environmental Conservation, available at: https://dec.vermont.gov/watershed/map/water-quality- standards. Page 2 of 7 Docusign Envelope ID: 34F80D10-74E9-42B7-A2AF-91F3BBD33BCA To the extent the EPA intends to adopt an interpretation that departs from decades of prior practice recognizing that certifying authorities may consider the activity as a whole, the EPA must acknowledge and justify that departure. Failure to provide a reasoned explanation for abandoning settled interpretations would render the final rule arbitrary and capricious. See FCC v. Fox Television Stations, Inc., 556 U.S. 502, 515 (2009) (explaining that an agency is required to “provide reasoned explanation for its action” and that an agency may not “depart from prior policy sub silento or simply disregard rules that are still on the books.”) EPA offers no reasoned explanation for abandoning decades of settled interpretation recognizing that certifying authorities may consider the activity as a whole and identifies no statutory basis for such a departure. II. Cooperative Federalism and State Sovereignty Congress expressly preserved broad state authority under the CWA through 33 U.S.C. § 1370. As mandated by Congress: [N]othing in this chapter shall… preclude or deny the right of any State…to adopt or enforce (A) any standard or limitation respecting discharges of pollutants, or (B) any requirement respecting control or abatement of pollution…[or] be construed as impairing or in any manner affecting any right or jurisdiction of the States with respect to the waters (including boundary waters) of such States. 33 U.S.C. § 1370 (emphasis added). Had Congress intended to permit EPA to restrict the scope of State regulation under Section 401 to only those requirements enumerated in the CWA, or to prohibit States from applying their own water quality laws to waters within their jurisdiction, it would have done so expressly. Instead, Congress used the unmodified, all-encompassing term “any,” signaling an intent to preserve broad State authority. As the Supreme Court has held, “read naturally, the word ‘any’ has an expansive meaning, that is, ‘one or some indiscriminately of whatever kind.’” Ali v. Federal Bureau of Prisons, 552 U.S. 214, 219 (2008). Where Congress employs such sweeping language, courts will not infer implied limitations absent clear statutory direction. Id. at 228. EPA’s proposal directly conflicts with § 1370’s statutory reservation of state authority. By suggesting states should be restricted to numeric criteria, monitoring requirements, or only EPA-approved provisions, the EPA seeks to narrow the scope of “water quality requirements” in a manner that prevents Vermont from applying its duly enacted standards. Such an interpretation exceeds EPA’s statutory authority and constitutes an ultra vires intrusion into state authority. See Utility Air Regulatory Group v. Page 3 of 7 Docusign Envelope ID: 34F80D10-74E9-42B7-A2AF-91F3BBD33BCA E.P.A., 573 U.S. 302, 325 (2014) (holding that the EPA “has no power to “tailor” legislation to bureaucratic policy goals by rewriting unambiguous statutory terms” as they must effectuate “’the unambiguously expressed intent of Congress’”); Arkansas v. Oklahoma, 503 U.S. 91, 107 (1992) (holding that “Congress, in crafting the [Clean Water] Act, protected certain sovereign interests of the States; for example, § 501 allows States to adopt more demanding pollution-control standards than those established under the Act.”) These defects manifest concretely in several aspects of the proposed rule. For example, EPA would bar states from considering impacts to non-federal waters or applying independent state water quality protections in Section 401 certifications. State review of federal general permits under Section 401 is necessary to ensure that activities authorized under such permits that may result in discharges to Waters of the United States comply with state water quality requirements. By effectively limiting “water quality requirements” to those aligned with EPA’s preferred scope of federal regulation, the proposed rule would prevent states from independently considering factors directly affecting their own waters and citizens. En masse, these restrictions would fundamentally alter the cooperative federalism structure Congress intentionally established by converting Section 401 from a state- driven safeguard into a federally constrained procedural formality. The CWA does not authorize the EPA to subordinate state oversight over water quality in this manner, and the proposed rule should not be finalized in a form that strips authority Congress expressly reserved for the states. III. Request for Certification In addition to improperly narrowing the substantive scope of certification, EPA’s proposal further undermines state authority by restricting the information certifying authorities may require from applicants in a request for certification. VTDEC disagrees with EPA’s apparent proposal to limit requests for certification to a defined list of components by removing the text in 40 C.F.R. § 121.5(c) which allows State and Tribal certifying authorities to define additional contents in certification requests. Vermont, as a certifying authority, is best equipped to determine what information is necessary to evaluate compliance with State water quality standards and other requirements of State law. Section 401 explicitly places the burden on an applicant to demonstrate compliance with state law. See 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a)(1) (requiring certification that the proposed activity will comply with state water quality requirements.) Nothing in the CWA Page 4 of 7 Docusign Envelope ID: 34F80D10-74E9-42B7-A2AF-91F3BBD33BCA limits a state’s ability to require information necessary to make that determination. To the contrary, the Court has repeatedly recognized that certifying authorities may require information sufficient to assess the environment effects of the proposed activity. See PUD No. 1, 511 U.S. at 713 (holding that “state water quality standards adopted pursuant to § 303 are among the “other limitations” with which a State may ensure compliance through the § 401 certification process”); S.D. Warren Co., 547 U.S. at 386 (holding that Congress expressly provided the States with power to enforce State law by regulating “activities that may result in a discharge,” not just discrete discharges); Arkansas v. Oklahoma, 503 U.S. at 103 (holding that Section 401 prohibits “the issuance of any federal license or permit over the objection of an affected State unless compliance with the affected State’s water quality requirements can be ensured”). The information necessary for a complete request varies significantly depending on project type, location, receiving waters, and potential impacts. A rigid federal checklist cannot account for site-specific conditions such as stream flow alterations, stormwater runoff, wetland impacts, or water withdrawals, all of which are routinely relevant under Vermont law. See 10 V.S.A. § 1253. By eliminating the authority of states to define additional request contents, EPA’s proposal would force certifying authorities to either deny certification based on incomplete information or issue certifications without adequate factual support. EPA’s proposal fails to identify any justification for improperly shifting the statutory burden from the applicant to the State and is inconsistent with both administrative law principles and the cooperative federalism structure of the CWA. IV. Modifications EPA’s proposed modification framework compounds these defects by conditioning state regulatory action on applicant consent and private negotiation. VTDEC disagrees with the EPA’s proposal to require applicant agreement as a precondition to modifying a certification. Further, EPA’s proposal would improperly subordinate Vermont’s regulatory authority to applicant consent and effectively grant regulated entities veto power over the enforcement of State water quality requirements. This approach directly conflicts with the plain text of Section 401, which provides that the certifying authority may grant, condition, or deny certification. See 33 U.S.C. §§ 1341(a)(1) & (d). Nothing in Section 401 authorizes EPA to subordinate State regulatory authority to applicant consent or to transfer decision- making power to regulated entities. The EPA may not create regulatory authority by implication, particularly where doing so restructures the federal-state balance. See Utility Air Regulatory Group, 573 U.S. at 324-25 (holding that it is “patently unreasonable—not Page 5 of 7 Docusign Envelope ID: 34F80D10-74E9-42B7-A2AF-91F3BBD33BCA to say outrageous” for an agency to claim expansive power the statute does not grant, particularly where such assertions of unheralded authority would radically expand the agency’s reach absent clear congressional authorization); Gregory v. Ashcroft, 501 U.S. 452, 460 (1991) (holding that where an agency interpretation would “alter the ‘usual constitutional balance between the States and the Federal Government,’ it must make its intention to do so ‘unmistakenly clear in the language of the statute.”) In addition to unlawfully shifting regulatory power away from states, this proposal replaces public, law-bound decision-making with private bargaining between regulators and regulated entities, in direct conflict with state laws that require certification decisions to occur through public, noticed, and independently administered regulatory processes. Under Vermont law 10 V.S.A. § 7717, any substantive modifications like major amendments are subject to public notice and comment, and VTDEC—as the certifying authority—retains discretionary authority to determine whether modification is warranted, notwithstanding the requirements of 40 C.F.R. § 121.10(a). This public noticing requirement by Vermont law provides necessary transparency and preserves independent decision-making as a certifying authority. Moreover, these proposed changes would have the practical effect of coercing states into relinquishing enforcement authority by conditioning regulatory action on applicant consent. EPA identifies no statutory authority for this framework, which is inconsistent with the structure of Section 401 and undermines authority Congress expressly reserved to the States. Absent a clear delegation from Congress, such a restructuring of the certification process is legally indefensible. Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. 369, 411-12 (2024) (explaining that an agency that changes positions with “unexplained inconsistency” warrants a holding that any such action is arbitrary and capricious) Had Congress intended to authorize EPA to delegate state regulatory authority to regulated entities, it would have done so expressly in statute. EPA’s proposal cites no statutory text or legislative history that would support such a delegation, and none appears to exist. Accordingly, the proposal to require applicant agreement as a precondition to modification exceeds EPA’s authority and should not be finalized. V. Conclusion For the foregoing reasons, EPA should abandon and withdraw this rulemaking. If you have questions or would like to discuss these comments, please contact me or the Vermont 401 Coordinator at Bethany.Sargent@vermont.gov or 802-490-6131. Page 6 of 7 Docusign Envelope ID: 34F80D10-74E9-42B7-A2AF-91F3BBD33BCA Sincerely, Misty Sinsigalli, Commissioner Vermont Department of Environmental Conservation Vermont Agency of Natural Resources Page 7 of 7
